role_hints.role: PR practitioners (account staff, media relations, content)
role_hints.oews_basis: 27-3031 Public Relations Specialists 8.3% of wage bill in the 4-digit 5418 proxy — within true PR agencies this function plus account tiers is the delivery core, est. ~30-40% of the actual wage bill including tail account staff
role_hints.note: Press releases, pitch drafts, media lists, coverage monitoring/reporting, bylines and social content are heavily AI-exposed; journalist relationships, crisis counsel in the room and executive trust do not automate. Plausible within-role fraction 0.35-0.55.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 9.3% + 11-2021 Marketing Managers 5.2% (proxy) + agency principals in the tail
role_hints.note: 6,209 of 8,157 firms have <5 employees — substantial owner-operator comp; client counsel and new business persist, internal management automates.

role_hints.oews_basis: 13-1161 Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists 6.6% (proxy)
role_hints.note: Media analysis, sentiment tracking and measurement reporting are already tool-heavy and AI-automate rapidly.

role_hints.oews_basis: 27-1024 Graphic Designers 3.3% + 27-1011 Art Directors 4.1% (proxy — partly contamination from 541810)
role_hints.note: Visual content production automates; brand-level creative direction persists at integrated shops.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3011 Advertising Sales Agents 8.2% + 41-3091 Sales Reps 3.0% in the proxy are mostly contamination from sibling codes; true PR biz-dev est. ~4-5%
role_hints.note: RFP responses and capability decks automate; relationship-driven wins do not.

role_hints.role: Back office, admin and remaining tail
role_hints.oews_basis: not in listed top-10; finance, HR, admin and misc. — the 4-digit top-10 covers ~51% of wage bill and includes contaminant occupations (41-9011 Demonstrators 1.6%, 27-1026 Merchandise Displayers 1.2%) that are NOT PR-agency jobs
role_hints.note: Billing, timesheets, clip reporting and scheduling — directly automatable overhead.

source_hints.source: Gould+Partners annual Billing Rates & Utilization / Best Practices Benchmarking reports
source_hints.why: The standard PR-agency profitability benchmark (billing rates, utilization, over-servicing levels) — direct evidence for whether AI hour-savings can be kept or are already given away as over-service.
Tracks AI adoption and attitudes across the PR profession — usable for current_adoption_pct.

source_hints.source: PRovoke Media (formerly Holmes Report) agency rankings and industry coverage
source_hints.why: Agency revenue rankings, M&A coverage and AI commentary for the sector.

source_hints.source: O'Dwyer's PR firm rankings and M&A coverage
source_hints.why: Long-running independent-PR-firm revenue data and deal reporting — consolidator identity and small-firm multiples.
Global PR market sizing, growth and consolidation context.

source_hints.source: Gould+Partners M&A and valuation commentary on PR firm sales
source_hints.why: Specific to founder-led PR firm exits — succession dynamics and typical deal structures (earn-outs dominate).

capture_questions: PR bills monthly retainers scoped against staff-hour plans and rate cards, with documented chronic over-servicing (Gould+Partners benchmarks it) — when AI cuts the hours a retainer covers, do clients rescope the retainer down at renewal, or does the agency keep the spread as recovered over-service? Which way is the evidence already pointing?

Who owns the client relationship — the firm or the founder/senior counselor? PR client books are notoriously portable with individuals; what do earn-out-heavy deal structures in this industry say about buyer beliefs on retention?

What stops clients from using generic AI for releases, content and media lists plus a junior in-house hire — is the remaining paid product only senior counsel and journalist access, and how many billable hours does that actually support?
Does measurement/analytics or specialized vertical expertise (healthcare, financial, crisis) create defensible pricing, or is generalist consumer PR the deflation-exposed bulk?
terminal_value_question: Nothing statutory protects PR; the load-bearing mechanisms are journalist relationships (human gatekeepers at shrinking newsrooms) and executive trust in crisis. Mature AI writes the releases, builds the lists, monitors coverage and drafts the strategy — and AI-generated pitch floods are already devaluing mass outreach. Does paid demand consolidate into a small senior-counsel-and-access business (product shrinkage) rather than a cheaper version of today's retainer book (cost cut)? Answer with evidence on retainer sizes and hours-per-account trends, not sentiment.
special_notes: Pricing-model deflation candidate: hour-scoped retainers with client-visible staffing plans are a direct deflation channel — and the industry's documented over-servicing (agencies delivering more hours than billed) means AI savings may first just reduce unbilled over-service before touching margin; model both stages in pi_dist.

EBITDA normalization: small-firm profit is SDE with owner comp inside; PR deals are earn-out-heavy precisely because client books walk with founders — quoted 'multiples' often describe structured maximums, not cash at close.

Role-mix contamination: the record's role_mix is the 4-digit 5418 proxy including ad-agency and media-rep occupations (ad sales agents, demonstrators, merchandise displayers) that do not exist in PR agencies — use the adjusted structure and say so.

Boundary confusion: integrated communications shops straddle 541820/541810/541613; investor relations and public affairs firms also sit here; association and ranking data (PRovoke, O'Dwyer's) is self-reported by firms spanning codes — check coverage.

Media-environment dependency: the earned-media product depends on a shrinking journalism sector — newsroom contraction is a secular demand headwind independent of AI; do not attribute all decline to AI when scoring.
